label: State Senator
subject: S447, H3155, and H4013 need a hearing — and they need amendments
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint when a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew their weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They are now suing Greenville PD.

This is what happens when surveillance technology runs without rules, and it's happening right here in South Carolina.

There are 242 Flock Safety cameras across Upstate SC. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country, without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Three bills are waiting for a hearing: S447, H3155, and H4013. All three are stalled in committee. They need to move, but they also need to be strengthened. As written, all three bills regulate what government agencies can do with camera data. None of them address what Flock itself does with it.

Flock operates the network. Flock holds the data on its own servers. Flock decides which agencies can query which cameras. A 90-day retention limit for police means nothing if Flock keeps the data indefinitely. A ban on government selling data means nothing if cross-jurisdiction search is a product feature Flock sells. Once data enters the network, local police cannot pull it back. No policy change fixes that.

Any bill that passes must be amended to:
- Require that private vendors like Flock delete data on the same schedule as law enforcement
- Give local jurisdictions the right to block outside agencies from querying their camera data through the vendor's network
- Prohibit vendors from granting federal access without explicit local authorization
- Require public disclosure of all data-sharing agreements between the vendor and any government entity

Without these provisions, the bills regulate the buyers but leave the seller unaccountable. Please push for a committee hearing and fight for these amendments.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: State Representative
subject: S447, H3155, and H4013 need a hearing — and they need amendments
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint when a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew their weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They are now suing Greenville PD.

There are 242 Flock Safety cameras across Upstate SC. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country, without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenville City Council
subject: Greenville needs a public vote on Flock Safety
body:
Dear [NAME],

Last year, two Greenville sisters driving a rental car were pulled over at gunpoint when a Flock Safety camera wrongly flagged their vehicle as stolen. Police drew their weapons. Both women were handcuffed. The car wasn't stolen. They are now suing Greenville PD.

Greenville PD runs 57 Flock Safety cameras. The first went up in 2021, paid for with civil asset forfeiture funds, with no council vote on oversight and no rules about who can see the data, how long it's kept, or who checks how it's used. Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police cameras across the country, without telling those agencies. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it three weeks later.

Once Greenville's data is in that network, the city can't pull it back. No policy change fixes that. It's how the system is built. None of that requires a warrant. The city's own camera contracts are designed to pull in private HOA cameras and residents' Ring cameras, growing the network beyond what's publicly visible with no additional vote.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, local action is the only check on this system.

The council has the power to change this. I'm asking you to:
- Hold a public hearing on Greenville's Flock Safety contract and how the data is actually shared
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before any new surveillance technology is deployed
- Refuse to expand the system until residents have had a real say

Sedona, AZ; Hays County, TX; and Cambridge, MA all looked closely at how Flock works and decided it wasn't worth it. Greenville residents deserve the same chance to weigh in.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenville County Council
subject: Greenville County needs oversight of Flock Safety cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Greenville County Sheriff's Office operates 11 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The City of Greenville runs 57 more. All of this data feeds into a shared network where SLED retains it for three years, and Flock has given federal agencies direct access without local agencies' knowledge.

The Sheriff's Office has no county-level oversight policy governing how this data is accessed, shared, or audited. Meanwhile, the county's SafeWatch program is pulling HOA and private cameras into the same network, expanding surveillance reach without any public vote or transparency.

Flock's CEO denied federal data sharing on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, county oversight is the only check on this system.

Last year in Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. They are now suing. This is what happens when surveillance technology runs without oversight.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the camera network

Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their Flock contracts after reviewing how the data sharing works. Greenville County residents deserve the same transparency.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Spartanburg City Council
subject: Spartanburg needs local oversight of surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Flock Safety license plate reader cameras are deployed in the Spartanburg area, tracking the movements of residents with no local oversight framework. The data feeds into a national network where Flock gave federal agencies direct access without telling local police departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later.

In May 2025, Spartanburg County Sheriff Chuck Wright pled guilty to federal charges of conspiracy to commit theft from federal programs and wire fraud. The official overseeing law enforcement surveillance deployments in this area was a federal criminal. That fact alone should prompt every elected body in Spartanburg to ask hard questions about who has access to residents' location data and under what rules.

There are 242 Flock cameras across Upstate SC. No city or county in the region has passed an oversight ordinance. Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with it on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, local action is the only check on this system.

I'm asking the City Council to:
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance camera deployments within city limits
- Require disclosure of all data-sharing agreements with Flock Safety
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before new surveillance technology is deployed

Communities like Sedona, AZ and Cambridge, MA reviewed how Flock works and decided the risks outweighed the benefits. Spartanburg residents deserve the same opportunity.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Spartanburg County Council
subject: County oversight is needed after Sheriff Wright's conviction
body:
Dear [NAME],

In May 2025, Spartanburg County Sheriff Chuck Wright pled guilty to federal charges of conspiracy to commit theft from federal programs and wire fraud. The county's own top law enforcement official, the person with access to Flock Safety license plate reader data tracking residents' movements, turned out to be a convicted criminal.

Flock cameras are deployed across Spartanburg County. The data feeds into a national network that Flock opened to federal agencies without telling local departments. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

The county has no oversight policy governing who accesses this data, how long it's retained, or how it's audited. Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its servers. Until the state acts, county oversight is the only safeguard. Given what happened with Sheriff Wright, the absence of safeguards is not an abstraction. A convicted criminal had unsupervised access to a system that tracks where residents drive.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require full disclosure of all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of surveillance contracts

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after learning how data sharing works. Spartanburg County residents deserve accountability, especially now.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Anderson City Council
subject: Anderson needs transparency on surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

In 2024, South Carolina's own Transportation Secretary discovered over 200 unpermitted Flock Safety cameras on state roads and confirmed the Legislature has never addressed the privacy concerns they raise. No city in the Upstate has established oversight of this technology. Anderson is no exception — the same cameras and the same national network operate here with no local rules.

Flock's CEO was asked on camera in July 2025 if the company had federal contracts. He said no. Three weeks later, he admitted Flock had secretly given Border Patrol access to local cameras nationwide. Once location data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

In neighboring Spartanburg, the sheriff who oversaw Flock camera deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges in May 2025. In neighboring Greenville, a Flock camera wrongly flagged an innocent woman's rental car and police drew their weapons. The consequences of surveillance without accountability are already at Anderson's doorstep.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with it on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, local action is the only check on this system.

I'm asking the City Council to:
- Disclose all Flock Safety deployments and data-sharing agreements within city limits
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance technology oversight
- Require council approval before deploying or expanding any automated surveillance system

Sedona, AZ and Hays County, TX reviewed how Flock works and cancelled their contracts. Anderson deserves the same transparency.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Anderson County Council
subject: Anderson County needs an oversight policy for surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

In neighboring Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw Flock Safety camera deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges in May 2025. The same cameras and the same national data network operate in Anderson County — with no oversight policy governing how the data is collected, shared, or retained.

Flock's CEO was asked on camera in July 2025 if the company had federal contracts. He said no. Three weeks later, he admitted Flock had secretly given Border Patrol access to local cameras nationwide. Once location data enters the Flock network, no local policy can retrieve it. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

South Carolina's own Transportation Secretary confirmed in 2024 that the Legislature has never addressed ALPR data privacy — after discovering over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras on state roads. In Greenville, a Flock camera wrongly flagged an innocent woman's rental car and police drew their weapons. No county in the Upstate has established any safeguards.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its servers. Until the state closes that gap, county oversight is the only safeguard.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require disclosure of all Flock camera deployments and data-sharing agreements in the county
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits and access controls
- Hold a public hearing before renewing or expanding any surveillance contracts

Hays County, TX and Cambridge, MA reviewed Flock's data practices and decided the risks were too great. Anderson County should do the same.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Pickens County Council
subject: Multiple agencies run Flock cameras in Pickens County with no coordinated oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Multiple agencies operate Flock Safety license plate reader cameras in Pickens County, including the Pickens County Sheriff's Office and Clemson University Police. Each deployment feeds into the same national Flock network, but there is no coordinated county-level oversight governing any of them.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the network, no local agency can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

With multiple agencies operating independently, no one is tracking the total number of cameras, who can access the combined data, how long it's kept, or whether the deployments overlap. Residents moving through Pickens County may be tracked by multiple systems with no coordinated rules.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its servers. Until the state closes that gap, county oversight is the only safeguard.

In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their car. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff overseeing surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. The Upstate has 242 Flock cameras and zero oversight policies.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require all agencies operating Flock cameras in Pickens County to disclose their deployments and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the camera network

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Laurens County Council
subject: Laurens County should act on surveillance oversight before cameras expand
body:
Dear [NAME],

Across Upstate South Carolina, 242 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras track where residents drive. The South Carolina Legislature has never addressed ALPR data privacy. No county in the region has an oversight policy. And the problems are already showing up in our neighboring counties.

Flock's CEO was asked on camera in July 2025 if the company had federal contracts. He said no. Three weeks later, he admitted Flock had secretly given Border Patrol access to local cameras nationwide without telling the departments involved. In Spartanburg, the sheriff who oversaw camera deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. In Greenville, a Flock camera wrongly flagged an innocent woman's rental car and police drew weapons. The consequences are already here.

Once location data enters the Flock network, no local policy can pull it back. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it. SLED retains the data for three years. Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with the data on its servers. Until the state closes that gap, county oversight is the only safeguard.

Laurens County has an opportunity to get ahead of this. Rather than waiting for problems to arrive, the council can establish clear rules now.

I'm asking the County Council to:
- Require disclosure of any current or planned surveillance camera deployments in the county
- Establish an oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and auditing before any new system is adopted
- Hold a public hearing so residents can weigh in

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Richland County Council
subject: The Richland County Sheriff quoted $9,000 to hide two CSV files from the public
body:
Dear [NAME],

In December 2025, a journalist asked the Richland County Sheriff's Department for two CSV files showing how the department uses its Flock Safety license plate reader cameras and which outside agencies access the data. The response: a bill for $9,152, a six-week timeline, and a demand for 25% upfront. Florence PD handed over the same records for free in one day. Bluffton PD mailed a USB drive for $20.

This is not an isolated problem. The Richland County Sheriff's Department was ordered to pay $6,000 in FOIA penalties in 2020 and previously ignored a FOIA audit request entirely. The department operates 5 Flock cameras. Columbia PD runs its own ALPR system through the SLED statewide database. And SLED itself — headquartered right here in Richland County — stores 422 million license plate reads with 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies.

Flock Safety's CEO was asked on camera in July 2025 whether his company had federal contracts. He said no. Three weeks later, he admitted Flock had secretly given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local police cameras without telling the departments involved. Once data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot pull it back.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but all three are stalled in committee and none regulate what Flock itself does with data on its servers. Until the state acts, county oversight is the only safeguard — and right now, Richland County has none.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Department to disclose all Flock camera locations and data-sharing agreements at no cost to the public
- Establish a county oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and regular auditing of ALPR use
- Require council approval before any expansion of automated surveillance technology

Denver, CO passed a surveillance oversight ordinance requiring public notice and city council approval before new systems go live. Richland County, sitting at the center of the state's ALPR infrastructure, should lead by example — not stonewall the public for $9,000.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Columbia City Council
subject: Columbia's Flock cameras are already failing the transparency test
body:
Dear [NAME],

In October 2024, Columbia City Council voted 5-2 to approve 130 Flock Safety cameras at a cost of $539,400 in the first year and $476,000 annually after that. Alongside that vote, the council unanimously approved a new oversight process for surveillance technology. It was the right instinct. But the oversight is already failing.

Columbia PD's Flock transparency portal does not display which police beats or wards have cameras. The department has not complied with the disclosure requirements the council itself established. Meanwhile, the cameras are live, the data is flowing to SLED's 422-million-read statewide database, and Flock's national network remains open to outside agencies.

Flock's CEO denied having federal contracts on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. The company had secretly given Customs and Border Protection access to local police cameras across the country without telling the departments involved. Columbia's 90-day data retention policy means nothing if Flock keeps the data longer on its own servers.

The council approved this technology with guardrails. Those guardrails are not working. That is fixable, but only if the council acts.

I am asking you to:
- Require Columbia PD to bring its Flock transparency portal into full compliance with the oversight ordinance, including camera locations by beat and ward
- Amend the Flock contract to explicitly prohibit federal agency access without city council authorization and ban use for immigration enforcement
- Conduct a public audit of how camera data has been accessed and shared in the first year of deployment

Austin, TX terminated its Flock contract after reviewing data-sharing concerns. Columbia does not need to go that far — but the council must enforce the oversight it already promised.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Charleston County Council
subject: A Charleston County deputy used surveillance cameras to stalk his wife
body:
Dear [NAME],

In June 2024, North Charleston Police Lt. Ryan Terrell admitted to using city-owned surveillance cameras to track his wife, whom he suspected of having an affair with a fellow officer. Data logs showed Terrell accessed camera feeds on multiple days, following her movements via live video until he confronted her in a Target parking lot. He was demoted but not fired.

That is what happens when surveillance infrastructure exists without meaningful oversight. And the infrastructure in Charleston County is vast. The Charleston County Sheriff's Office operates 25+ Flock cameras and expanded coverage to James Island in May 2024. Charleston PD runs 12 Flock cameras. Mount Pleasant PD participates in the SLED statewide ALPR database. The Charleston County SO also operates DataWorks Plus facial recognition technology — crowd-scanning capabilities deployed since 2013.

All of this data feeds into networks where Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access without telling local departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. SLED retains 422 million plate reads for three years.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none regulate what Flock itself does with data on its servers. Until the state closes that gap, county oversight is the only check.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require disclosure of all ALPR and facial recognition deployments, costs, and data-sharing agreements across county agencies
- Establish a county surveillance oversight policy with mandatory audit trails and penalties for misuse
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of surveillance technology contracts

Santa Cruz, CA banned government use of facial recognition and established strict oversight of all surveillance technology. Charleston County has facial recognition and ALPR systems running with no comparable safeguards.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Charleston City Council
subject: Charleston needs surveillance oversight before the next expansion
body:
Dear [NAME],

Charleston PD operates 12 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The Charleston County Sheriff's Office runs 25 more and recently expanded coverage to James Island. Mount Pleasant PD is connected through the SLED statewide database. For anyone driving through the Charleston peninsula and surrounding areas, these overlapping systems track their movements across multiple agencies with no unified oversight.

In neighboring North Charleston, a police lieutenant used city surveillance cameras to stalk his wife in 2024. He was demoted but kept his job. That abuse was only discovered because someone checked the access logs — something no Charleston oversight policy currently requires.

Flock Safety's CEO denied federal contracts on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. The company had given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling the departments involved. Once data enters the Flock network, Charleston PD cannot control who searches it. SLED's statewide database holds 422 million plate reads with 3-year retention.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None of them address what Flock does with the data on its own servers. Until the legislature acts, local oversight is the only protection.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Charleston PD to publish a public transparency portal showing camera locations, access logs, and data-sharing partners
- Pass a surveillance technology ordinance requiring council approval before any new automated surveillance system is deployed or expanded
- Hold a public hearing on data-sharing agreements between Charleston PD, the county, and Flock Safety

Staunton, VA cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised concerns about unregulated data sharing. Charleston residents deserve the same opportunity to weigh in.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: North Charleston City Council
subject: Lt. Terrell proved why $2.5M in surveillance needs real oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

In June 2024, North Charleston Police Lt. Ryan Terrell — the officer who oversaw the city's technology unit, including its Verkada camera system — admitted to using city-owned surveillance cameras to track his wife's movements on multiple days. He followed her via live video and confronted her in a Target parking lot. He was demoted to patrol officer and given six months probation. He was not fired.

North Charleston has invested $2.5 million in 800+ Verkada surveillance cameras, 34 Rekor ALPR units, a Fusus real-time crime center that can pull feeds from private cameras, and a Joint Operations Center at City Hall. The council approved an additional $1.5 million for the Joint Operations Center buildout in 2025. Annual operating costs will reach $2.2 million after year five.

This is the largest municipal surveillance system in the state, and an officer already abused it. The system was built to integrate private cameras from businesses and residents, expanding surveillance reach far beyond what public cameras alone provide. There is no public oversight framework governing who accesses this data, how it is audited, or what consequences exist for misuse beyond a demotion.

Flock Safety's CEO denied federal contracts in July 2025 and admitted weeks later that the company had given Border Patrol access to local cameras nationwide. The ALPR data from North Charleston feeds into SLED's 422-million-read statewide database.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Establish a mandatory audit and access log system for all surveillance cameras, with annual public reporting
- Create clear penalties for surveillance misuse that go beyond demotion
- Require council approval before adding private cameras to the city's Fusus network or expanding the ALPR system

Eugene, OR rejected a Flock proposal after residents organized against unregulated surveillance. North Charleston has already built the system. The least the council can do is build the rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Mount Pleasant Town Council
subject: Mount Pleasant's ALPR data feeds a network with no local rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

Mount Pleasant PD operates Flock Safety license plate readers connected to the SLED statewide ALPR database, which stores 422 million plate reads with 3-year retention. The department also runs Motorola CityProtect, a camera registry system. There is no public transparency portal, no published use policy, and no town council oversight framework governing how this data is collected, shared, or retained.

Across the Cooper River, the Charleston County Sheriff's Office runs 25+ Flock cameras. Berkeley County SO operates 56 — the highest county sheriff count in the state. North Charleston has invested $2.5 million in 800+ cameras and 34 ALPR units. In June 2024, a North Charleston lieutenant was caught using city cameras to stalk his wife. Anyone driving between Mount Pleasant and these neighboring jurisdictions passes through multiple overlapping ALPR zones with no coordinated oversight.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it publicly in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once data enters the network, no local policy can retrieve it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its own servers.

I am asking the Town Council to:
- Require Mount Pleasant PD to publish camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Pass an ordinance requiring town council approval before any new surveillance technology is deployed
- Establish a data retention limit and mandatory audit trail for all ALPR data

Cambridge, MA established strict transparency and oversight requirements for surveillance technology. Mount Pleasant should not wait for a scandal to do the same.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Berkeley County Council
subject: 56 Flock cameras and zero public oversight — Berkeley County leads the state in the wrong way
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Berkeley County Sheriff's Office operates 56 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. That is the highest count for any county sheriff's office in South Carolina — more than ten times Richland County's five cameras and nearly double Charleston County's 25+. Despite this scale, no council vote authorized the deployment, no public hearing was held, and no transparency portal exists.

The 56 cameras are just the beginning. Goose Creek PD, Moncks Corner PD, Hanahan PD, Jamestown PD, and St. Stephen PD all participate in the SLED ALPR database. This gives Berkeley County near-total coverage on its major roads, with all data flowing to SLED's 422-million-read statewide database and Flock's national network.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to these camera networks without telling local departments. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. In neighboring North Charleston, a police lieutenant was caught using surveillance cameras to stalk his wife. Combined with Berkeley County's 56 cameras and North Charleston's 800+, the tri-county region has one of the densest surveillance networks in the state — with no governing legislation.

Sen. Brian Adams (R-Berkeley) spoke in support of cameras during the S.447 debate. But supporting the technology is not the same as overseeing it. Three state bills are stalled, and none regulate what Flock does with data on its own servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to publish all camera locations and data-sharing agreements in a public transparency portal
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and annual auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any further expansion of ALPR cameras in the county

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after learning how data sharing actually works. Berkeley County residents deserve to know that 56 cameras are tracking their movements before they learn it from a news article.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Goose Creek City Council
subject: Goose Creek is connected to 56 county cameras with no local rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

Goose Creek PD is a confirmed Flock Safety data-sharing partner, meaning it can query the Berkeley County Sheriff's 56-camera ALPR network — the largest county sheriff deployment in South Carolina. The city recently opened a new public safety hub that may house surveillance coordination. Yet there is no public transparency portal, no published data-sharing policy, and no city council oversight framework.

The data flowing through these systems does not stay local. SLED stores 422 million license plate reads with 3-year retention. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local camera networks without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Once data enters the network, Goose Creek cannot control who accesses it.

Across the region, North Charleston has invested $2.5 million in 800+ cameras, and a lieutenant there was caught using the system to stalk his wife. Charleston County SO runs 25+ Flock cameras. The tri-county area has one of the densest surveillance networks in the state with no unified oversight.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its own servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Disclose all data-sharing agreements between Goose Creek PD and outside agencies, including Berkeley County SO and Flock Safety
- Establish a city surveillance policy requiring council approval before adopting or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Set data retention limits and mandatory access logging for any ALPR data Goose Creek officers query

Sedona, AZ reviewed how Flock's data-sharing network operates and cancelled its contract. Goose Creek residents deserve to know what data is being collected about them and who can search it.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Moncks Corner Town Council
subject: Moncks Corner sits inside the state's largest county ALPR network
body:
Dear [NAME],

Moncks Corner PD participates in the SLED statewide ALPR database. The Berkeley County Sheriff's Office — headquartered here in Moncks Corner — operates 56 Flock Safety cameras, the highest count for any county sheriff's office in South Carolina. Every major road in and around Moncks Corner is monitored. There is no public oversight framework.

The data collected does not stay in Berkeley County. SLED retains 422 million plate reads for three years, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling the departments involved — a fact their CEO denied on camera in July 2025 and admitted three weeks later.

In the broader tri-county region, North Charleston has spent $2.5 million on surveillance infrastructure, and a police lieutenant there was caught using cameras to track his wife's personal movements. The absence of oversight is not hypothetical — it has already led to documented abuse in a neighboring city.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled and none regulate Flock's own data practices.

I am asking the Town Council to:
- Request a full briefing from the Berkeley County Sheriff's Office on camera locations, data retention, and outside agency access
- Establish a town policy governing Moncks Corner PD's participation in shared surveillance networks
- Hold a public hearing before any new surveillance technology is adopted by the town

Denver, CO requires public notice and city council approval before any new surveillance system goes live. Moncks Corner should not be the county seat of the state's most-surveilled county without any public input.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Horry County Council
subject: 108+ Flock cameras, 38.7 million plate reads, and no county oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Horry County agencies operate at least 108 Flock Safety cameras. Myrtle Beach PD's Real Time Crime Center logged 38.7 million plate reads in 2019 alone and now provides over 1,900 camera views. Conway PD runs 54 cameras for a city of 25,000 — one camera for every 460 residents. North Myrtle Beach is building a $2 million Real Time Crime Center of its own. There is no county-level oversight governing any of it.

These cameras do not just serve local investigations. All data feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users. Flock Safety secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local camera networks without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Myrtle Beach PD claims a 47% decrease in Part 1 crime since 2015 and credits the cameras. But a National Institute of Justice study of Detroit's similar Project Green Light found no evidence that cameras reduced violent crime. Meanwhile, the millions of tourists visiting the Grand Strand each year have their plate data captured and stored without any knowledge or consent.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with the data on its own servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Conduct a county-wide inventory of all ALPR deployments, costs, and data-sharing agreements across every agency in Horry County
- Establish a county surveillance oversight policy with data retention standards and mandatory auditing
- Require council approval before any agency expands its ALPR network or enters new data-sharing agreements

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after reviewing how data sharing works. Horry County has more cameras than most Texas counties ever considered — and zero oversight.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Myrtle Beach City Council
subject: 38.7 million plate reads a year deserves more than a press release
body:
Dear [NAME],

Myrtle Beach PD's Real Time Crime Center recorded 38.7 million license plate reads in 2019 alone. The system now provides over 1,900 camera views. The FY 2025-2026 budget allocates $300,000 for camera repairs and new phases, and 118 new camera views were added around The Market Common in 2024 with 250 more planned for 2025. The department also runs ShotSpotter at $90,000 per year and has a Ring doorbell partnership giving police access to private residential footage.

This is one of the most extensive municipal surveillance systems in South Carolina. Yet there is no public transparency portal, no city council oversight ordinance, and no published policy governing who can access the ALPR data or how long it is retained.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years. Millions of tourists visit Myrtle Beach each year — their plate data is captured, stored, and searchable by agencies they have never heard of.

The department claims a 47% drop in Part 1 crime since 2015 and credits the cameras. But independent research — including a National Institute of Justice study of a comparable system in Detroit — found no evidence that cameras reduce violent crime.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Commission an independent audit of the Real Time Crime Center's data collection, retention, and sharing practices
- Pass an ordinance requiring council approval before expanding surveillance technology or adding private cameras to the city network
- Establish a public transparency portal with camera locations, access policies, and annual reporting

Austin, TX terminated its Flock contract after reviewing privacy concerns. Myrtle Beach has a larger system than most cities that have taken action. The council should ensure residents and visitors know how their data is being used.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Conway City Council
subject: 54 cameras for 25,000 people — Conway deserves a public conversation
body:
Dear [NAME],

Conway PD operates 54 Flock Safety cameras as of September 2025. For a city of approximately 25,000, that works out to roughly one camera for every 460 residents. The department also recently installed ShotSpotter gunshot detection. There has been no public hearing, no city council vote on oversight, and no published policy governing how the ALPR data is collected, stored, or shared.

That data does not stay in Conway. It flows into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later.

Conway's 54 cameras sit within a broader Horry County surveillance corridor. Myrtle Beach PD logged 38.7 million plate reads in a single year. North Myrtle Beach is building a $2 million Real Time Crime Center. Anyone driving between Conway and the coast passes through multiple overlapping ALPR zones.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but all are stalled and none regulate Flock's own data practices.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Hold a public hearing on Conway PD's Flock camera deployment, including camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass an ordinance requiring city council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance systems
- Establish a data retention limit shorter than SLED's 3-year default and publish a use policy

Staunton, VA cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised questions about data sharing. Conway residents have not even had the chance to ask.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Florence County Council
subject: Sheriff Joye is putting cameras on private property to dodge state rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

When SCDOT paused permits for Flock cameras on state road rights-of-way in 2024, Florence County Sheriff T.J. Joye found a workaround: installing cameras on private property adjacent to public roads. Homeowners volunteered their land, and the sheriff told reporters they just want to help catch criminals. He had 76 Flock cameras in patrol cars and 14 stationary units he wanted to deploy.

This tactic effectively circumvents a state regulatory pause that exists because SCDOT's own Secretary discovered over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras on state roads and raised unresolved privacy concerns. The Florence County legislative delegation even introduced H5146 to force SCDOT to cooperate with the county's camera installations.

Meanwhile, the Sheriff's Office retains ALPR data for one year, while Florence PD deletes after 30 days. The same county, two agencies, two different retention policies — because there are no standards. All of this data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read database and Flock's national network, where the CEO lied about giving federal agencies access.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all camera locations, including those on private property, and all data-sharing agreements
- Establish a uniform county data retention standard for all agencies operating ALPR cameras
- Require council approval before any further expansion of surveillance camera deployments, including the private property workaround

Sedona, AZ and Hays County, TX both cancelled Flock contracts after examining how the data actually flows. Florence County is expanding faster than almost any county in the state. The council should know what it is approving.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Florence City Council
subject: A $400,000 state grant bought 30 cameras — and no oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Florence PD's 30 Flock Safety cameras were funded by a $397,500 state legislative appropriation secured by Sen. Mike Reichenbach — not from the city budget. The cameras cover most gateways in and out of the city, including the Highway 76 corridor. The data auto-deletes after 30 days from Florence PD's system. But it does not auto-delete from SLED's statewide database, which stores 422 million plate reads for three years, or from Flock's national network.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Florence PD's 30-day retention means nothing if the data lives elsewhere for years.

The Florence County Sheriff's Office operates an additional 14 stationary cameras and 76 mobile units, with data retained for one year. The sheriff is installing cameras on private property to bypass SCDOT's permit pause. Florence is building a surveillance grid from both city and county directions with no coordinated oversight.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's own data handling.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Amend Florence PD's Flock contract to explicitly prohibit federal agency access without city council authorization
- Require annual public reporting on camera locations, alert types, data-sharing partners, and access logs
- Hold a public hearing on whether the current 30-camera deployment should expand, contract, or stay the same

Cambridge, MA adopted strict transparency requirements and access controls for surveillance technology. Florence's cameras were funded by a state grant with no strings attached — the city council should attach some.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Beaufort County Council
subject: 83 million plate scans in one year — and the sheriff says he can track you across the country
body:
Dear [NAME],

In its first year of operation, the Beaufort County Sheriff's ALPR cameras captured 83 million license plate images. Officers conducted 4,320 individual searches — about 11 per day. Sheriff PJ Tanner told the Island Packet: "For the most part, we can track you all over the country."

Beaufort County has invested over $600,000 in nearly 70 ALPR cameras, using both Rekor and Flock Safety vendors simultaneously. The Sheriff's Office chose Rekor; Beaufort PD, Bluffton PD, and Hilton Head use Flock. Councilman David Bartholomew was the sole dissenter on the ARPA funding request, asking what this technology would be used for 10 or 15 years from now. He said he could not support spending federal relief funds on surveillance without state regulation.

Councilman Bartholomew was right to ask. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years. Hilton Head Island alone draws millions of visitors whose plate data is captured without their knowledge.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and all municipal agencies to publish unified data-sharing agreements and access policies
- Establish a county surveillance oversight committee with civilian representation to review ALPR use annually
- Adopt a county ordinance requiring council approval before expanding surveillance technology or entering new vendor contracts

Santa Cruz, CA banned government facial recognition and imposed strict surveillance oversight. Beaufort County is running two separate vendor systems that together scan 83 million plates a year. That scale demands accountability.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Beaufort City Council
subject: Beaufort PD's Flock cameras feed a network the city does not control
body:
Dear [NAME],

Beaufort PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The data feeds into Flock's national network and SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users. The Beaufort County Sheriff's Office runs a separate system using Rekor cameras that captured 83 million plate images in its first year. Between the city and county systems, Beaufort residents are tracked by two competing vendor networks with different data handling practices.

Flock Safety's CEO denied federal contracts on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. The company had given Customs and Border Protection direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. Beaufort PD cannot control who accesses data once it enters the Flock network.

Sheriff Tanner has publicly stated he can track vehicles across the country using these partnerships. That is not a local policing tool. It is a mass surveillance network operating without local rules.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what vendors do with data on their servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Beaufort PD to publish a transparency portal showing camera locations, data-sharing partners, and access logs
- Pass an ordinance setting data retention limits and requiring council approval before expanding surveillance technology
- Coordinate with the county to establish unified oversight standards across all agencies operating ALPR cameras in Beaufort

Eugene, OR rejected a Flock proposal after residents organized against the lack of oversight. Beaufort already has cameras deployed — the city should at least build the oversight framework that Eugene demanded before deployment.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Hilton Head Island Town Council
subject: Millions of visitors' plate data is being captured with no town oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Hilton Head Island uses Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. The Beaufort County Sheriff's Office operates a separate system with Rekor cameras that captured 83 million plate images in its first year. Sheriff PJ Tanner has publicly stated he can track vehicles across the country. Hilton Head draws millions of visitors annually — every one of their license plates is being scanned, stored, and made searchable across a national network.

There is no town oversight framework. No published policy governing data retention, access, or sharing. No public transparency portal. No town council vote authorizing the deployment or setting rules for how tourist and resident data is handled.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO lied about it publicly. SLED retains 422 million plate reads for three years. For a tourism economy, the reputational risk of unregulated mass surveillance should concern every elected official.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set some rules but are stalled and do not regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the Town Council to:
- Require a full accounting of all ALPR cameras operating on Hilton Head Island, their costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass a town ordinance requiring council approval and public notice before any surveillance technology is deployed or expanded on the island
- Establish a data retention limit and publish a clear policy governing access to visitor and resident plate data

Folly Beach, just up the coast, operates one of the few public Flock transparency portals in the state, with published policies and a 30-day retention limit. If a small beach town can manage transparency, Hilton Head can too.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: York County Council
subject: 58+ cameras across six agencies and no county-level oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

York County has at least 58 Flock Safety cameras spread across the County Sheriff's Office, Rock Hill PD, Fort Mill PD, Tega Cay PD, Clover PD, and the Catawba Nation. Tega Cay has gone so far as to create a "virtual gate" — cameras positioned to log every vehicle entering and leaving the city. Six agencies, no coordinated oversight, and no public discussion about who can access the combined data.

All of this feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once data enters the network, no local agency can control who searches it.

York County's proximity to Charlotte means residents regularly cross state lines. The ALPR data collected here does not respect jurisdictional boundaries — it flows into national networks that link South Carolina cameras to systems in North Carolina and beyond.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's own data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Conduct a county-wide inventory of ALPR deployments across all six agencies, including camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with unified data retention standards and mandatory auditing
- Require council approval before any agency in the county expands its surveillance camera network

Denver, CO requires public notice and city council approval before new surveillance systems are deployed. York County has six agencies operating independently with no comparable safeguard.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Rock Hill City Council
subject: Rock Hill needs oversight before surveillance cameras expand further
body:
Dear [NAME],

Rock Hill PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras as part of a York County network that includes at least 58 cameras across six agencies. Tega Cay has created a "virtual gate" logging every vehicle that enters or leaves the city. Rock Hill residents driving through the county are captured by multiple overlapping systems with no unified rules.

All of this data flows into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Once the data enters the network, Rock Hill PD cannot control who searches it.

Rock Hill's location near the North Carolina border means cross-state commuters have their plate data captured daily and stored in systems that link South Carolina and North Carolina surveillance networks. There is no public transparency portal, no city council oversight policy, and no published rules about data retention or access.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Rock Hill PD to disclose all camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass an ordinance requiring city council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Establish a public use policy with data retention limits and mandatory access logging

Staunton, VA cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised concerns about unregulated data sharing. Rock Hill residents deserve a say before the network grows further.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Newberry County Council
subject: 40 Flock cameras for 38,000 residents — the council should know why
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Newberry County Sheriff's Office operates 40 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras. For a county of approximately 38,000 residents, that is one of the highest per-capita ALPR deployment rates in South Carolina. There has been no public hearing, no county council vote on oversight, and no transparency portal showing where these cameras are or who can access the data.

All 40 cameras feed into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras nationwide without telling departments. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. Once data enters the network, Newberry County cannot control who searches it.

In neighboring Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. The Upstate and Midlands have hundreds of cameras and zero oversight policies.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's own data handling.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all 40 camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular public auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any renewal or expansion of the Flock contract

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after examining data-sharing practices. Newberry County's 40 cameras represent a significant investment in surveillance for a small community — residents deserve to know the terms.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Newberry City Council
subject: The county has 40 cameras — Newberry needs local oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Newberry County Sheriff's Office operates 40 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras across the county. That is one of the highest per-capita deployments in South Carolina. Residents driving through the city of Newberry are captured by this network, and the data flows into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. No local policy can retrieve data once it enters the Flock network. There is no city or county oversight framework governing how this data is used, retained, or audited.

The Newberry City Council has the authority to set rules for its own jurisdiction, even where the county has not. Cities across the country have acted when their states and counties failed to.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Request a briefing from the Sheriff's Office on how the 40-camera network affects Newberry residents
- Pass a city ordinance requiring council approval before any surveillance technology is deployed within city limits
- Establish a data retention limit and public use policy for any ALPR system the city may adopt in the future

Sedona, AZ cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised concerns. Newberry should set rules before cameras arrive in city hands, not after.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenwood County Council
subject: 65 cameras across three agencies — who is overseeing the network?
body:
Dear [NAME],

Greenwood County has approximately 65 Flock Safety cameras spread across the County Sheriff's Office, Greenwood City PD, and Lander University PD. Three agencies, three separate deployments, no coordinated county-level oversight. There is no public transparency portal, no published data-sharing policy, and no county council framework governing how this combined network operates.

All of this data feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. Once data enters the Flock network, no local agency can retrieve it.

With three agencies operating cameras independently, no one is tracking the total number, who can access the combined data, or whether the deployments overlap. Residents may be captured by multiple systems on the same road without anyone coordinating how that data is used.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require all three agencies to disclose their camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements to the council and the public
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access, and auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any agency in the county expands its ALPR deployment

Cambridge, MA established comprehensive oversight requirements for surveillance technology, including public reporting and elected body approval. Greenwood County's three-agency network needs at minimum a coordinated set of rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Greenwood City Council
subject: Greenwood PD runs Flock cameras with no public rules — the council can fix that
body:
Dear [NAME],

Greenwood PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras as part of a county network totaling approximately 65 cameras across three agencies — the County Sheriff's Office, Greenwood City PD, and Lander University PD. The county has no oversight policy, and the city has none either. There is no public transparency portal, no published data retention policy, and no city council framework governing who can access the data or how it is shared.

The data goes well beyond Greenwood. SLED stores 422 million plate reads with 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments — a fact their CEO denied publicly before admitting it weeks later. Once data enters the network, Greenwood PD cannot pull it back. No local policy change fixes that.

Across the Upstate, the consequences of unregulated ALPR use are already visible. In Greenville, a Flock camera wrongly flagged an innocent woman's rental car and police drew weapons on two sisters who were handcuffed on the side of the road. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance camera deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. Over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras were found on state roads by SCDOT's own Secretary.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's data practices. Until the state acts, local rules are the only check.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Greenwood PD to disclose all camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass a city surveillance ordinance requiring council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Establish data retention limits and mandatory access logging for ALPR data

Austin, TX terminated its Flock contract after reviewing privacy and data-sharing concerns. Greenwood should establish rules before the system grows further.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Chester County Council
subject: 25+ cameras in a county of 32,000 — the public deserves a say
body:
Dear [NAME],

Chester County has at least 25 Flock Safety cameras operated by the County Sheriff's Office and Chester PD combined. For a county of approximately 32,000 residents, that is roughly one camera per 1,280 people — one of the higher ratios in the state. There has been no public hearing, no county council vote on oversight, and no transparency framework.

All of this data feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later.

Chester County sits on the I-77 corridor between Columbia and Charlotte. Every commuter and through-traveler has their plate logged, stored for years, and searchable by agencies they have never heard of. No local policy governs any of it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled in committee. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and Chester PD to disclose all camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing before any expansion of the ALPR network

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after learning how data sharing works. Chester County residents driving the I-77 corridor deserve to know their movements are being tracked — and under what rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Chester City Council
subject: Chester PD's cameras feed a network the city does not control
body:
Dear [NAME],

Chester PD operates Flock Safety license plate reader cameras as part of a county network totaling at least 25 cameras. The data does not stay local. It feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads stored for three years, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO lied about it publicly before admitting the truth weeks later.

Chester sits on the I-77 corridor between Columbia and Charlotte. Every commuter, every traveler, every resident driving through town has their plate captured by cameras that the city council never formally authorized or set rules for. There is no published data retention policy, no transparency portal, and no public oversight framework. The data collected on this interstate corridor connects to surveillance networks in both directions — linking South Carolina systems to those in North Carolina and beyond.

Flock's network is designed to share data across jurisdictions. That is its core product. A camera in Chester captures a plate, and within seconds that data is searchable by agencies the city has never heard of. Federal agencies do not need a warrant to query it. Over 200 unpermitted Flock cameras were found on state roads by SCDOT's own Secretary, and three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) that would set basic rules are stalled in committee. None of them regulate what Flock does with the data on its own servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Chester PD to disclose all camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Pass a city ordinance setting data retention limits and requiring council approval before any surveillance technology is deployed or expanded
- Request a public briefing on how ALPR data collected in Chester is shared with outside agencies

Denver, CO requires elected body approval and public notice before any new surveillance technology goes live. Chester is a small city on a major corridor — it should have oversight that matches the scale of data flowing through its cameras.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Sumter County Council
subject: The House Speaker's law firm defends SLED's database — Sumter County needs its own oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Sumter County Sheriff's Office operates 30 Flock Safety cameras. Sumter PD is listed as a data-sharing partner on the Flock network. All of this feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users. There is no county oversight policy.

Sumter has a unique connection to this issue. Smith Robinson, a Sumter-based law firm, is leading SLED's defense in the SCPIF v. SLED lawsuit challenging the legality of the state's license plate database. One of the firm's co-founding partners is Murrell Smith Jr., the Speaker of the South Carolina House of Representatives. Three ALPR bills (S447, H3155, H4013) have stalled in the legislature he leads, while his firm defends SLED's authority to operate the database without legislative authorization.

Regardless of how that lawsuit plays out, the county council has authority to set local rules. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Shaw Air Force Base sits adjacent to Sumter, and SLED's database has been accessed from military installations statewide.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all 30 camera locations and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits, access controls, and regular auditing
- Hold a public hearing on ALPR use in Sumter County, including questions about federal and military access to local data

Santa Cruz, CA established strict oversight of surveillance technology with civilian review and public reporting. Sumter County sits at the intersection of state politics and federal military presence — the residents deserve local accountability.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Sumter City Council
subject: Sumter PD shares data with a network no one locally oversees
body:
Dear [NAME],

Sumter PD is listed as a Flock Safety data-sharing partner, meaning the department can query camera data from agencies across the region and the country. The Sumter County Sheriff's Office operates 30 Flock cameras of its own. All of this data flows into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies.

There is no city oversight framework. No published use policy. No transparency portal. No city council vote authorized these data-sharing arrangements or set rules for how the data is retained, audited, or accessed by outside agencies.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Shaw Air Force Base, one of the largest Air Combat Command installations in the country, sits adjacent to Sumter. SLED's database has been accessed from military installations statewide. The question of whether military or federal law enforcement access Sumter's camera data has no public answer — and no local policy requires one.

Meanwhile, the Speaker of the South Carolina House, whose Sumter-based law firm is defending SLED in the lawsuit challenging the statewide database, has not moved any of the three ALPR bills (S447, H3155, H4013) through committee. Until the state acts, local rules are the only safeguard.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require Sumter PD to disclose all data-sharing agreements and publish a public use policy for ALPR data
- Pass an ordinance requiring city council approval before deploying or expanding automated surveillance technology
- Request an accounting of which agencies — local, state, and federal — have accessed Sumter's camera data

Eugene, OR rejected a Flock proposal after community members demanded answers about data sharing. Sumter residents should have the chance to ask the same questions.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Orangeburg County Council
subject: Two ALPR vendors, tiny-town police with Flock, and 1,000 campus cameras — who is watching the watchers?
body:
Dear [NAME],

Orangeburg County runs two competing ALPR vendors simultaneously. The city's Department of Public Safety uses Flock Safety with 29 cameras. The County Sheriff's Office uses NDI Recognition Systems. Branchville (population 900), Cameron (population 400), and Elloree (population 600) — three of the smallest municipalities in the state — also participate in Flock's national data-sharing network. And Claflin University operates 4 Flock cameras plus 144 traditional surveillance cameras, with plans to add facial recognition.

Two vendors means residents' data flows into two separate commercial systems with different retention policies and sharing agreements. No county-level body coordinates how these overlapping systems operate. All of it feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. Orangeburg County's two HBCUs — SC State and Claflin — bring a younger, more transient population subject to this surveillance infrastructure with no electoral power to influence it.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Conduct a county-wide audit of all ALPR and surveillance camera deployments, across all vendors, agencies, and institutions
- Establish a unified county oversight policy covering data retention, access controls, and public reporting
- Hold a public hearing with input from university communities and small-town residents affected by the surveillance network

Cambridge, MA requires public reporting and elected body approval for all surveillance technology. Orangeburg County's patchwork of vendors and agencies needs at minimum a single set of rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Orangeburg City Council
subject: 29 Flock cameras, 144 campus cameras, and no city oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Orangeburg Department of Public Safety operates 29 Flock Safety cameras. Claflin University runs 4 more, plus 144 traditional surveillance cameras with plans to reach 200 and add facial recognition at a cost of $300,000. SC State has planned 700 cameras of its own. The city and its campuses are blanketed in surveillance — and there is no city council oversight framework.

Each shooting near the campuses has accelerated camera deployment. The August 2022 Buckley Street shooting, the January 2024 Claflin science building incident, the February 2026 SC State residential complex shooting — after each one, more cameras with less debate. But cameras have not prevented the violence. They have expanded a surveillance system that tracks everyone, including the 4,000+ students who have no vote in Orangeburg city elections.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. All data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read database. Orangeburg's poverty rate is 23.9%, well above the state average — this community can least afford to be experimented on.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate vendor data practices.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Require the Department of Public Safety to publish camera locations, data-sharing agreements, and annual access reports
- Pass a city surveillance ordinance requiring council approval before expanding ALPR or adding facial recognition
- Include university and community representatives in a public hearing on surveillance oversight

Austin, TX terminated its Flock contract after reviewing data-sharing concerns. Orangeburg's cameras have not stopped the violence — but they are tracking every resident and student. The city should at least set the rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Union County Council
subject: 23 cameras for 26,700 people — one of the highest ratios in the state
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Union County Sheriff's Office operates 23 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras for a county of approximately 26,700 people. That is roughly one camera for every 1,160 residents — a higher per-capita rate than Greenville PD's 57 cameras for 72,000 people. This investment is significant for a county with a median household income of $41,200 and a 17.9% family poverty rate. No public hearing was held. No county council vote authorized an oversight framework.

All 23 cameras feed into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention, 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it three weeks later. Once data enters the network, Union County cannot control who searches it.

Union County has no interstate, no major university, and a declining population. Yet it has one of the densest ALPR deployments in the state relative to population. The privacy implications are the same regardless of county size: every driver's movements are logged, stored, and shared across a national network.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's data practices.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office to disclose all 23 camera locations, costs, and data-sharing agreements
- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits and mandatory access auditing
- Hold a public hearing so residents can weigh in on whether this level of surveillance is appropriate for the community

Sedona, AZ — a town of 10,000 — cancelled its Flock contract after reviewing data-sharing practices. Union County is not too small for oversight. It may be too small to afford surveillance without it.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Union City Council
subject: The county has 23 cameras tracking Union residents — the city should set its own rules
body:
Dear [NAME],

The Union County Sheriff's Office operates 23 Flock Safety cameras — one of the highest per-capita ALPR deployments in South Carolina for a county of 26,700. Union city residents are captured by this network every time they drive. The data feeds into SLED's 422-million-read statewide database with 3-year retention, accessible to 2,000+ users across 99+ agencies.

Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. There is no city or county oversight framework. No transparency portal. No public use policy.

The county council has not acted. But Union city has the authority to set rules for its own jurisdiction. If the city ever adopts its own ALPR system — or if county cameras already cover city streets — there should be a policy in place before, not after.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Request a briefing from the county on how the 23-camera network affects Union city residents
- Pass a city ordinance requiring council approval before any automated surveillance technology is deployed within city limits
- Establish a public use policy with data retention limits should the city ever operate its own cameras

Hays County, TX cancelled its Flock contract after examining data-sharing practices. Union is a small community, but the surveillance network it is part of is not small. The city should have rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Oconee County Council
subject: The Sheriff's Association president runs Oconee's cameras — the council should run the oversight
body:
Dear [NAME],

Oconee County Sheriff Mike Crenshaw operates 6 Flock Safety cameras and serves as President of the South Carolina Sheriffs' Association. He testified before the state Senate in favor of ALPR technology, citing a 2025 kidnapping case where Flock cameras helped recover a 9-year-old girl in Edgefield County. It was a legitimate success story.

But legitimate successes do not eliminate the need for oversight. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it publicly and admitted it weeks later. SLED stores 422 million plate reads for three years. Oconee County sits on the I-85 corridor at the Georgia border — a drug interdiction chokepoint where federal agencies like the DEA, CBP, and HSI operate. The question of whether those agencies access Oconee's camera data has no public answer.

Seneca PD runs 4 additional Flock cameras. Pendleton PD is listed on the Flock data-sharing network. No public hearing has been held. No county oversight policy exists.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate what Flock does with data on its servers.

I am asking the County Council to:
- Require the Sheriff's Office and Seneca PD to disclose all camera locations and data-sharing agreements
