- Establish a county oversight policy with data retention limits and mandatory access auditing
- Request an accounting of which agencies — including federal — have accessed Oconee County's ALPR data

Denver, CO requires public notice and elected body approval before surveillance technology is deployed. Sheriff Crenshaw's cameras may do good work — but the council, not the sheriff, should set the rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Walhalla City Council
subject: Oconee County has cameras on I-85 — Walhalla should set rules before they reach city limits
body:
Dear [NAME],

Oconee County Sheriff Mike Crenshaw operates 6 Flock Safety cameras across the county. Seneca PD runs 4 more. Pendleton PD is listed on the Flock data-sharing network. Walhalla does not currently operate its own ALPR system, but the county's cameras track vehicles throughout the area, and the data feeds into SLED's statewide database — 422 million plate reads, 3-year retention.

Sheriff Crenshaw, who also serves as President of the South Carolina Sheriffs' Association, has been a vocal advocate for the technology. But advocacy is not oversight. Flock Safety gave federal agencies direct access to local cameras without telling departments. Their CEO denied it in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Oconee County sits on the I-85 corridor at the Georgia border, where DEA and other federal agencies conduct drug interdiction operations.

Walhalla has an opportunity to act before cameras arrive in city hands. Setting rules now is easier than unwinding a system later.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) are stalled. None regulate Flock's data practices.

I am asking the City Council to:
- Pass a city ordinance requiring council approval and public notice before any automated surveillance technology is deployed within city limits
- Request a briefing from the county on how the existing camera network affects Walhalla residents
- Establish a public use policy with data retention limits for any future ALPR system

Sedona, AZ — a small community not unlike Walhalla in scale — cancelled its Flock contract after residents raised concerns. It is easier to build oversight into a system before it exists than to bolt it on after.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]

label: Local Representative
subject: Our community needs oversight of surveillance cameras
body:
Dear [NAME],

Across Upstate South Carolina, 242 Flock Safety license plate reader cameras track where residents drive. No city or county in the state has established a public oversight policy for this technology. The South Carolina Legislature has never addressed ALPR data privacy.

Flock secretly gave federal agencies direct access to local police camera networks across the country without telling the departments involved. Their CEO denied it on camera in July 2025 and admitted it weeks later. Once location data enters the Flock network, local agencies cannot retrieve it. Federal agencies don't need a warrant to search it.

This isn't hypothetical. In Greenville, two sisters were handcuffed at gunpoint after a Flock camera wrongly flagged their rental car as stolen. In Spartanburg, the county sheriff who oversaw surveillance deployments pled guilty to federal criminal charges. SLED retains license plate data for three years with no public accountability framework.

Three state bills (S447, H3155, H4013) would set rules for police use of camera data, but none of them regulate what Flock itself does with it on its own servers. Until the state closes that gap, local oversight is the only check on this system.

Communities across the country have reviewed how Flock works and taken action. Hays County, TX and Sedona, AZ cancelled their contracts. Cambridge, MA established strict oversight rules. South Carolina has done neither.

I'm asking you to:
- Require disclosure of any surveillance camera deployments and data-sharing agreements in our community
- Hold a public hearing on surveillance technology oversight
- Establish a policy requiring elected body approval before new automated surveillance systems are deployed

Residents deserve to know who is tracking their movements and under what rules.

Sincerely,
[Your name and address]
